data.title: BNY Mellon Worldwide Growth Fund, Inc. Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
“ We anticipate heightened focus on both the implications of new political leadership around the world and changes in global central bank policies in the coming quarters.”

Market Review

The MSCI World Index was down modestly in the fourth quarter of 2024, generating a return of -0.2%. Over the period, key themes included consistently strong corporate earnings reports, global central banks’ continued monetary easing policies, and the resulting implications of a busy election year that ushered in sweeping political changes around the world. The different economic outlooks in the U.S., which remained strong, and in Europe and Asia, which remained more challenged, also impacted investor sentiment. This divide manifested in regionalized equity market performance with U.S. stocks delivering gains while European and Asian stocks delivered negative returns in the quarter.

Reports from management teams throughout the earnings season relayed the familiar dynamics of a resilient but weakening consumer as well as elevated corporate spending on artificial intelligence (AI) infrastructure investment. Many consumer-facing companies reported slowdowns in spending, which were offset by promotional discounting and price cuts. While the market reacted positively to the announcement of a more pronounced stimulus strategy by the Chinese government during the quarter, a commensurate uplift in Chinese consumer spending has yet to come and companies continued to report lackluster results from the region. Investors also remained focused on returns generated by high levels of AI infrastructure investment.

Major central banks around the world continue to ease monetary policy by cutting key interest rates. The U.S. Federal Reserve (Fed) cut the Federal Funds Rate by 25 basis points (bps) both in November and December. Market participants viewed these cuts as supportive of growth. Investor enthusiasm was somewhat dampened when the Fed’s updated forecast projected 50 bps of rate cuts in 2025, down from their previous forecast of 100 bps. A reduced easing cadence hinted at the Fed’s concern of reemergent inflation, and investors also remain focused on the potentially inflationary nature of President-elect Donald Trump’s proposed fiscal policies. The European Central Bank delivered two rate cuts, while the Bank of England issued a rate cut in its November meeting and then held rates steady in its December meeting. While rates broadly remain in restrictive territory, global central banks have generally adopted gradual, data-dependent approaches to easing.

Performance Summary

The BNY Mellon Worldwide Growth Fund (MUTF:PGROX)(MUTF:DPWRX) underperformed the MSCI World Index in the fourth quarter of 2024.

Trump’s U.S. presidential election win was more decisive than expected, and the lack of a drawn out or litigated election result was welcomed by investors. Investors cheered on the prospects of corporate tax cuts and deregulation as positive for corporate earnings and U.S. economic growth.

In contrast to the optimistic outlook for America, a more challenged outlook for Europe and Asia reflected concerns of economic stagnation, the specter of trade wars, geopolitical tensions, and political uncertainties. In December, France’s new Prime Minister was replaced amid disagreement over the country’s budget and Germany called for snap elections amid concerns of an economic slowdown. Likewise, consternation arose throughout Asia after the U.S. election, as the likelihood of new tariffs and more protectionist policies from America may hurt Asia’s export dependent economies.

Performance Review

The Fund trailed the Index in the quarter, as a negative selection effect outweighed a positive allocation effect. The Fund’s relative performance was supported by its holdings in the financials and communication sectors, which resulted in positive selection effects. The underweight allocation to the materials sector also contributed positively to relative results. Conversely, negative stock selection effects across the information technology and industrial sectors outweighed positive allocation effects, resulting in relative results that lagged the Index. The Fund’s relative overweight allocation and negative stock selection effect in the consumer staples sector also detracted from relative performance.

Top Contributors

The top contributors to relative performance include Apple, Amazon.com, Alphabet, Visa, and JPMorgan Chase (JPM).

Top Detractors

The top detractors from relative performance include Novo Nordisk, LVMH, L’Oreal (OTCPK:LRLCF)(OTCPK:LRLCY), BAE Systems (OTCPK:BAESF)(OTCPK:BAESY), and ASML (ASML).

Market Outlook

We anticipate heightened focus on both the implications of new political leadership around the world and changes in global central bank policies in the coming quarters. Many of the world’s major economies, including the U.S. have ushered or will be ushering in regime change. Volatility and uncertainty may persist as equity markets adjust to shifts in trade policies, foreign relations, domestic fiscal spending, and monetary policies.

Over the past year, the world’s major central banks have pivoted towards monetary easing. We expect that monetary policies will continue to be accommodative as central banks move towards neutral rates, facilitating conditions supportive of growth as both consumers and businesses have access to cheaper credit. Risk assets, such as stocks, could become relatively more attractive as fixed income yields decline. However, the gradual pace of easing may mean that an uptick in inflation remains a risk. We continue to monitor for any signs of increased inflation or other stressors to the economy.

The Fund’s investment philosophy remains disciplined during periods of economic and political change. While government policies might change, we remain steadfast in seeking out what we consider to be high quality industry leaders that are headed by exceptional management teams, demonstrate the ability to grow earnings, and maintain strong balance sheets throughout economic cycles. We believe companies with these characteristics may be able to better adapt and take advantage of changing policies, while being financially sound enough to absorb any potential shocks. We believe our unwavering investment approach enables investors to participate meaningfully in periods of market expansion while also potentially benefiting from defensive characteristics in periods of market volatility and uncertainty.

Risks

Equities are subject to market, market sector, market liquidity, issuer, and investment style risks to varying degrees. Small and mid-sized company stocks tend to be more volatile and less liquid than larger company stocks as these companies are less established and more volatile earnings histories. Investing in foreign denominated and/or domiciled securities involves special risks, including changes in currency exchange rates, political, economic, and social instability, limited company information, differing auditing and legal standards, and less market liquidity. These risks generally are greater with emerging market countries. A concentration of companies in a narrow sector could cause performance to be more volatile than funds invested in a broader range of industries.

Index Definitions

The MSCI World Index is designed to measure global equity performance of developed markets. The Index includes 23 MSCI national developed market indices. Investors cannot invest directly in any index.

data.title: RPAR Risk Parity ETF: The Path To 8% Annual Returns Or More
data.description:
There is no sugar-coating it: my track record on multi-asset class and diversified investing has been dismal in the past five years. I first issued a "strong buy" rating on the RPAR Risk Parity ETF (NYSEARCA:RPAR) in July 2020. Since then, distributions included, the fund has remained flat (i.e., no meaningful gains or losses) while the S&P 500 (SPY) doubled in value, as depicted below.

It turns out that betting on a stock-and-bond combo, the "meat and potatoes" of the risk-parity ETF, since 2021 has yielded among the worst three-year returns in history. While I cannot guarantee that things will improve substantially in the immediate future, I have reasons to believe that RPAR could deliver high single-digit, if not low double-digit returns per year over the next decade at least. RPAR and its close cousin UPAR Ultra Risk Parity ETF (UPAR) remain a strong buy, in my view.

A quick word on RPAR

For the benefit of those unfamiliar with risk parity, the strategy aims to combine assets that have low correlations in an attempt to capture the free-lunch effect of diversification in an efficient manner. Efficient, in this case, means investing less in high-volatility assets, and more in low-volatility ones. Doing so, theoretically, better balances the portfolio by not overly exposing it to the riskiest holdings (usually stocks).

Since allocating more capital to less volatile asset classes naturally results in lower expected returns, RPAR makes up for it by leveraging its total exposure to the underlying assets at a factor of around 1.2 times (for UPAR, the leverage factor is 1.7 times, while all else is very much the same). Below is a visual representation of RPAR's target asset and risk allocations.

RPAR: Bad news (past), good news (future)

On paper, being balanced across domestic and international stocks, bonds, precious metals, and commodity producers sounds like a reasonable, relatively safe way to invest. This has been the case throughout the past century.

For simplicity, think of a portfolio that invests 70% in ten-year treasuries; 40% in the S&P 500; and 10% in gold (notice the 1.2x leverage at play here). This allocation would roughly emulate RPAR's target asset allocation depicted above. In the 90 years between 1932 and 2021, such a portfolio would have returned a respectable 9.1% per year, with the worst year at -13.2% in 1937 being substantially better than the S&P 500's -36.6% in 2008.

But in 2022, the strategy would have delivered a whopping -19.6%, the second-worst performance since the 1920s. On a three-year rolling basis, the negative returns seen between 2021 and 2023 would have only been better than the three-year periods that ended in 1931, 1932, and 1941 (see chart below).

The above is a reminder of how unusual it is for a diversified asset class strategy to perform so poorly as it has recently -- driven, primarily, by a massive and still lasting bear market in bonds following the post-COVID spike in inflation and interest rates. It is also a subtle argument for why things should improve from here. Judging by the historical data alone, I would expect future returns in RPAR to improve substantially and more closely match the 9% or so annualized returns seen over the long run.

My beliefs are further supported by CAPM (the capital asset pricing model). With the risk-free rate today hovering around 4.5%, and assuming a market risk premium of 5%, I would expect the S&P 500 to return around 9.5% per year going forward, give or take a couple of percentage points and the expected month-to-month ebbs and flows in stock prices.

In addition, as I have presented in the past on Seeking Alpha, returns in treasuries and treasury funds over the life of the underlying security (i.e., ten years in the case of ten-year treasuries) approximate their yield. That is: investing in intermediate-term US government bonds today should return approximately 4.5% in the next one or two decades.

If the above proves to be true, a 1.2x leveraged strategy could return 7% per year over the longer term, possibly 8% if gold prices climb by about 10% per year from here. This already respectable rate of return could be higher, and possibly reach double-digit territory, if bond yields finally begin to moderate from their current levels, as they were expected to until recently.

Final remarks on RPAR

Of course, investing in risky assets (stocks, bonds, commodities, and anything that is not cash) comes with uncertainty. It is impossible to categorically affirm that leveraged risk parity will finally produce the moderate-to-high returns that are expected of it, especially in the shorter term.

But relative to single-asset bets (e.g., all-in on the S&P 500), I think that owning RPAR today makes a lot of sense from a risk-reward perspective -- even though I have been burned by this ETF and by diversified asset class investing at large in the past few years.

data.title: Graham Corporation: Slightly Weak Results But Overreaction Creates A Buying Opportunity.
data.description:
Introduction

I initiated a buy rating on Graham Corporation (NYSE:GHM) last month and since then, the stock has underperformed the S&P 500 index by falling ~9% as compared to the S&P 500 increasing ~6% at the same time. Although the stock was trending up post my initial coverage, it tanked post the earnings announcement on February 7, 2025.

On the surface, GHM has reported mixed numbers; however, the fall in order intake made the markets uneasy. I believe the lower order intake is a part and parcel of GHM’s business, as much of its orders are lumpy in nature and one should not conclude slow demand from low order intake from just a single quarter. Nevertheless, the stock price has tanked due to this concern, which has widened the return expectation and margin of safety on the stock. Hence, I have retained my initial buy rating on the GHM.

3Q25 recap

GHM’s 3Q25 result give or take was somewhat better than expected on the bottom-line front, but the topline was a slight miss. The company reported topline growth of ~7.3% YoY as compared to the consensus estimate of ~13% growth. On the bottom-line front, the company beat the consensus non-GAAP EPS estimate by $0.11 and reported $0.18 of non-GAAP EPS.

On the surface, the topline miss looks a bit disappointing, but it is just normal for a business like GHM. GHM's sales cycle includes booking an order, then working on it and then transferring these orders to its customers, resulting in revenue. Mostly these orders take a couple of quarters to materialize in sales. Although there are mechanisms like over-time recognition and the percentage of completion method to recognize sales regularly, there are times when the timing of revenue recognition tends to make the growth figure a bit lumpy, which makes it an unreliable indicator for the underlying growth of the company. In this quarter’s context, the project timing issue relating to the refining segment led to the slower topline growth. In addition, the third quarter is typically the slowest quarter among all the quarters due to the holiday season being in the quarter, resulting in further slowness in topline growth.

As mentioned earlier, sales growth does not reflect the true underlying picture of GHM’s business; order growth tends to give a good leading picture of how the company is performing. For the 3Q25, the order growth was not that encouraging as well, dropping significantly from the 3Q24 order intake of $123 million to a meager $24.8 million level. The primary reason for such a large drop was due to a significantly tough comparison of prior year’s quarter, which was fueled by large follow-on orders by the US Navy in 3QFY24. Moreover, one could also see how lumpy the order flows had been for GHM in the last 3 years.

I believe viewing GHM’s future prospect with a dip in order intake for a single quarter should be unjust for the company. However, the overall trend for the last few quarters should be factored in the analysis, which is still positive. While the order intake was a weak spot for the quarter, its YTD book-to-bill ratio is still near 1x. Since the company plans to grow 8-10% organically over the long term, I believe the book-to-bill ratio should trend toward 1.1x in the coming quarters.

On the profitability front, GHM reported a 260 bps improvement in gross margins to 24.8%. The gross margins are now starting to trend near the 25%+ long-term gross margins guidance. While gross margins improved 260 bps, its leverage was not visible in adjusted EBITDA margins, which improved by just 180 bps. This was due to higher SG&A expenses due to investment in people, processes, and technologies and implementation of a new ERP system in the Batavia facility. This adjusted EBITDA number also includes higher employee expenses to the Barber Nichols business unit, which totaled to ~$1.1 million, or 230 bps of total revenue. This extra compensation should wear off starting from 2026 and adjusted EBITDA margins should start to trail near low teens post-wearing off of this expense item.

Overall, the results were a little off on the order growth front, which, I believe, is just a timing and lumpiness of orders issue. Margin expansion is on target and provides a runway for operational leverage going forward. With respect to guidance, the management has maintained all the guidance it has given earlier, except guidance for gross margins and SG&A. Management has guided for a 100 bps better gross margins now than earlier and a 100 bps higher SG&A cost now than earlier. The net impact on the adjusted EBITDA margins should remain negligible.

Valuation

I do not feel anything much has changed on the fundamental basis from my last coverage on GHM but the price has corrected a little. Since fundamentals are the same, I would use similar assumptions to project EBITDA as used earlier.

Assumptions

Revenue: 8-10% revenue growth as guided by management, not including growth from acquisitions, which is highly likely due to a good balance sheet and management’s intent to grow inorganically.

EBITDA margins: Assumed in line with management guidance and a little lower for FY27 (management guidance is for low to mid teens).

EV/EBITDA multiple: Multiple has been slightly modified as the peer comparison multiples have been changed.

Net Debt: Assumed to be constant over the years even though GHM would generate cashflow going forward.

After considering these assumptions, I have arrived at a price target of ~$60 per share for FY27, which is 55% higher than the current market price of its stock.

Risk

GHM has reported a slower order intake for 3Q25, although the order intake at times is lumpy but continued slower order intake in subsequent quarters should be seen as a change in business dynamics. Moreover, the actions taken by the Department of Government Efficiency [DOGE] should also be monitored carefully, as DOGE is aimed at cutting costs in US shipbuilding.

Conclusion

The recent result was neither that positive nor is it a clear negative for the company. According to my understanding, the fundamentals are still intact for the company and the slowness in order intake is just the nature of GHM’s business. However, the market has taken it as a clear negative, knocking the stock price more than it deserves. This fall has increased my margin of safety and return expectation from the stock. Due to this, I am retaining the buy rating on the GHM.

In our view, the probability of a recession in 2025 remains low due to robust corporate profits, strong labor markets and tight credit spreads.

Market Review

Equity markets were mixed in the final quarter of 2024 as the re-election of Donald J. Trump and rate cuts buoyed US equities, while tariff threats and slowing economic growth pressured other markets. For the quarter, the S&P 500® Index added 2.41%, while the MSCI EAFE Index, a measure of non-US developed markets, declined - 8.11% and the MSCI Emerging Markets Index declined -8.01%. Artificial intelligence (AI) once again drove growth stocks to handily outperform their value peers while large-cap stocks bested their small-cap counterparts.

Quarterly Performance

In the US, market advances were fueled by rate cuts in November and December, as well as hopes of pro-business policies and promises of tax cuts by President-elect Trump. However, gains were tempered in December after the US Federal Reserve (Fed) signaled the potential for fewer rate cuts in 2025. Market breadth again narrowed during the quarter with over half of the sectors in the S&P 500 posting losses. In addition to AI-fueled gains in the communication services, information technology and consumer discretionary sectors, financial stocks advanced on the hopes of a more regulatory-friendly environment under the new presidential administration. Conversely, the materials sector continued to be hampered by slowing growth in China, while health-care stocks faced multiple headwinds following the election.

Economic growth remained solid, buoyed by resilient consumer spending. The 10-year US Treasury yield ended the quarter higher, and the US dollar surged on election results and President-elect Trump’s pro-US economic agenda.

Developed international markets fell as European stocks plummeted around interest-rate uncertainty and fears of tariffs under the new US presidential administration. With inflation moving closer to target, the European Central Bank reduced interest rates twice. However, economic growth remained sluggish, political strife continued in France and the German government collapsed. In Germany, the region’s largest economy, both consumer and business sentiment suffered and the risk of recession rose over the quarter. In the UK, the Bank of England cut rates in November but then held them steady in December given growing inflation forecasts. Core consumer inflation in Japan remained above the central bank's 2% target; however, the Bank of Japan left rates unchanged.

Emerging market equities retreated for the quarter, facing pressure from a sluggish Chinese economy, a strengthening US dollar and concerns over potential tariffs. While Chinese equities rallied in December, they ended the quarter lower on concerns over economic growth. Authorities have stepped up measures to support the economy, including rate cuts, relaxed home-buying restrictions, purchases of sovereign bonds and multi-billion injections into outright reverse repurchase agreements. India’s economy slowed in the third quarter due to weaker expansion in manufacturing and decreasing consumption. In Brazil, stocks underperformed amid worries over the government's commitment to cut public spending and reign in the growing budget deficit.

Commodities advanced for the quarter, bolstered by natural-gas prices. An early and frigid start of winter in North America and Europe, in addition to the end of Russian supplies to Europe, pushed prices higher. Weak demand from China and surplus concerns continued to pressure crude oil. While starting the quarter higher, gold prices lagged due to the strengthening US dollar, the US election results and the dissipation of tensions in the Middle East. The Dow Jones Commodity Index ended the quarter up by 0.49%.

Performance Review

The fund (Class A shares at NAV) outperformed its benchmark, the Russell 2500 Growth Index, during the fourth quarter of 2024. From a sector perspective, consumer discretionary led relative contributors over the period, followed by communication services. Conversely, industrials and consumer staples were the most significant laggards.

Sector Review

Positive Impacts

Consumer Discretionary: Consumer discretionary led relative contributors over the period, largely driven by stock selection among the hotels, restaurants & leisure space. In particular, Dutch Bros Class A (BROS) was a top contributor after reporting third quarter results, which exceeded expectations, and raised full year guidance.

Communication Services: Stock selection among communication services also contributed to relative returns, particularly among the interactive media & services segment. Our holding of Reddit Class A (RDDT) buoyed returns after a solid quarter as machine translation continues to be a daily active user driver. Additionally, being in the public market has helped Reddit’s notoriety with potential new users as well. The market continues to be positive on the company’s ability to pull engagement levers and improve its internal search capabilities.

Negative Impacts

Industrials: Industrials led relative detractors by sector over the period. Stock selection among the trading companies & distributors segment and the building products segment weighed on results. In particular, our holding of Builders FirstSource (BLDR) was a top detractor as fundamentals remain challenged in the housing market, but we expect this to change next year as the Fed cuts rates and the industry recovers.

Consumer Staples: Consumer staples also detracted from relative returns over the period, primarily due to an overweight to the beverages space.

Stock Review1

Key Contributors – Q4 24

Reddit Class A: Reddit operates as an online discussion platform. The company publicizes online items recommended by members and aggregates news stories submitted by users. Shares of Reddit Class A had a solid quarter as machine translation continues to be a daily active user driver. Additionally, being in the public market has helped Reddit’s notoriety with potential new users as well. The market continues to be positive on the company’s ability to pull engagement levers and improve its internal search capabilities.

Axon Enterprise: Axon Enterprise integrates a suite of hardware devices and cloud software solutions. The company's three product categories include TASER, sensors and software. Axon performed well as the company continues to deliver a strong platform of hardware and software solutions to global public safety end markets. Axon is at the forefront of enabling and capturing the digitalization of evidence, an important secular theme we have identified over the next decade.

Dutch Bros Class A: Dutch Bros operates and franchises drive-thru beverage shops in the United States. Shares of Dutch Bros rose over the period after reporting third quarter results, which exceeded expectations, and raised full year guidance.

Key Detractors – Q4 24

TransMedics Group (TMDX): TransMedics manufactures and sells an organ transplant system, allowing a donor organ to be monitored and infused in transport so it is more viable when it arrives at the implant hospital. Shares weighed on results over the quarter after the company reduced its fourth quarter and 2025 growth rate due to lower transplant volumes serviced.

Builders FirstSource: Builders FirstSource is a supplier and manufacturer of building materials, manufactured components and construction services to professional homebuilders, sub-contractors, remodelers and consumers. The company offers an integrated solution to its customers by providing manufacturing, supply and installation of a full range of structural and related building products. Shares fell as fundamentals remain challenged in the housing market, but we expect this to change next year as the Fed cuts rates and the industry potentially recovers.

Inspire Medical Systems: Inspire Medical Systems develops and markets a device that reduces sleep apnea by stimulating the hypoglossal nerve that connects with the tongue. This procedure is specifically for patients with obstructive sleep apnea derived from tongue obstruction, not lateral obstruction. Inspire underperformed in the fourth quarter owing to a re-rate that occurred in the stock in the third quarter. The market is bearish on near term company prospects owing to their 2025 launch of their next generation sleep apnea implant which may delay procedures in the fourth quarter of 2024 and the first quarter of 2025. Patients and doctors may want to wait for the new upgraded implant, thus reducing sales results for these two near-term quarters. As longer-term investors, we do not get mired in the short-term market concerns when the long-term outlook for tongue-based sleep apnea prevalence remains high and Inspire has, in our opinion, a strong product to treat it. Our estimates indicate that just 6% of the available patient pool has been penetrated.

Market Outlook

Predicting the outlook for the equity markets is challenging given a projected earnings growth of almost 13% in 2024 and the S&P trading at 21.6 times forward earnings, combined with expectations for more Federal Reserve (Fed) rate cuts despite inflation. While the Fed initiated rate cuts this past September, the bond market is signaling to investors that it does not buy the lower inflation story or weakening economy, as evidenced by 10-year yields that have only risen since then. Simultaneously, economic surprise indices have declined and are not contributing to higher yields. As the central bank grapples with balancing economic growth and inflation control, its actions may have far-reaching implications. In response to the evolving economic landscape, the Fed may opt for a range of policy measures. A continuation of the shift to accommodative monetary policies, such as lower interest rates, may be at risk given the resilience of the economy and inflation. If inflationary pressures persist, the Fed might change course and move to adopt a more hawkish stance by either holding current rates steady or even raising interest rates to temper economic overheating. This could lead to increased market volatility as investors adjust their expectations. Sustained higher interest rates would result in increased borrowing costs for businesses and consumers, potentially dampening economic growth and corporate profits.

Trump's 2.0 agenda blends pro-market and populist policies. We are optimistic about potential deregulation and tax cuts, while bond markets are wary of inflation and fiscal deficits. Success may depend on balancing trade, immigration and deficit policies, as well as resolving geopolitical conflicts. From an investment perspective, DOGE (Department of Government Efficiency) represents a notable development from Washington, presenting an opportunity to address fiscal issues that could lead to higher risk-free interest rates irrespective of inflation levels.

Advancements in this area may not generate widespread enthusiasm in Washington, D.C., which typically resists change.

In our view, the probability of a recession in 2025 remains low due to robust corporate profits, strong labor markets and tight credit spreads. However, given that the S&P is currently trading at nearly 22 times forward earnings, the Chicago Board Options Exchange's Volatility Index (VIX) stands at around 17, and Bitcoin hovers around the $100,000 milestone, it is evident that expectations for risk assets remain elevated. With the largest 10 stocks comprising almost 39% of the S&P 500, much of the active large-cap managers' outperformance this year could hinge on the duration of the artificial intelligence (AI) trade's success. While we are optimistic about the continued success of the AI trade, we remain vigilant. Companies' ability to manage costs and maintain profit margins may be under scrutiny. One of the important highlights for 2025 may be the monetization of the broad AI efforts and spend over the last two years. Other factors such as supply-chain disruptions and labor-market tightness could exert upward pressure on costs, challenging businesses to find efficiencies and pass on price increases to consumers.

Geopolitical factors are set to play a significant role in shaping the equity outlook for 2025. Key areas of focus include tensions between major economies, regional conflicts and international trade policies. China's economic policies and performance is expected to have significant implications for global markets in 2025. The Chinese government could continue its focus on achieving stable growth while addressing structural challenges, such as debt levels and demographic shifts. Policies aimed at stimulating domestic consumption and reducing reliance on exports may support economic resilience. However, regulatory interventions in key sectors and geopolitical tensions could introduce uncertainty. We will be closely monitoring developments in China's property market, technological sector and trade relations with key partners, including the US. The relationship between the US and China could be particularly influential, with ongoing trade negotiations and strategic competition impacting market dynamics. Any progress toward de-escalation or cooperation could provide a boost to global equities, while heightened tensions could lead to market volatility. In Europe, the political landscape should be closely watched as countries navigate post-Brexit adjustments and address economic challenges, such as energy security and fiscal stability. Emerging markets, too, could present both opportunities and risks, with investors weighing growth potential against political and economic stability.

data.title: Dividend Harvesting Portfolio Week 207: $20,700 Allocated, $2,010.09 In Projected Dividends
data.description:
2025 continues to be an exciting year as the S&P 500 climbed 1.06% while the Nasdaq increased by 1.74% this week. We're only 2 weeks into February, and the S&P 500 is already up 3.96% YTD, and my prediction that the market will reach 7,000 by the end of 2025 doesn't look as crazy as it did during the end of 2024. If the market climbs another 15% this year, the S&P will exceed 7,000, and we will have 3 more earnings seasons before the end of 2025. CPI came in a bit hotter than expected, but it didn't derail the market. I think the critical thing to look at is that the index for shelter rose 0.4%, accounting for nearly 30% of the increase in CPI. The FOMC minutes come out this week, and it will be interesting to see what they discussed. I don't believe the Fed is only going to cut 2 times in 2025, as the only way to get shelter down at this point is to lower rates. We need an environment where the cost of capital is lower, and landlords can refinance at lower rates. If that doesn't happen, I do not see a solution to stabilize or lower rents. I am confident that the economy will be strong in 2025, and it will be interesting to see what President Trump announces as part of his tax plan for corporations. I think there are still a lot of opportunities in the market, and sectors such as REITs should do well as rates trend lower.

At some point, the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio will experience a retracement, but until then, I am going to enjoy these types of weeks. In week 207, the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio reached a new milestone in terms of the total account value and amount of dividend income being generated. After allocating $20,700 to the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio, the account balance finished in week 207 at $26,630.67. The account's profitability increased by $233.81 to $5,930.67, which is a return on invested capital of 28.65%. This week, I added to my positions in Pfizer Inc. (PFE), Starwood Property Trust, Inc. (STWD), and the Reaves Utility Income Trust (UTG). The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio generated $16.76 of dividend income in week 207. The combination of reinvesting the generated dividend income and making these investments increased the forward projected annualized dividend income by $8.60 (0.43%) to $2,010.09. New milestones are being reached, and I think that 2025 will be an exceptional year for the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio as I continue to allocate capital and reinvest all the dividends that are produced.

The Overall Performance of the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio

Since week 1, I have stuck to my investment rules of investing $100 per week and not letting any positions exceed 5% of the portfolio or any sector exceed 20% of the portfolio. This has forced me to allocate my invested capital across many positions, which, I believe, has helped me achieve my investment goals of generating recurring income while mitigating downside risk. Capital appreciation is a secondary goal that I am trying to generate as a byproduct of my first 2 goals. When I look at the chart below, I see that this strategy has allowed me to withstand macroeconomic headwinds, geopolitical tensions, and a rising rate environment quite well. The portfolio is now up $5,930.67 (28.65%) and is generating $2,010.09 in forward dividend income, which is a yield of 9.71% on invested capital and 7.55% on the current portfolio value. I am pleased with the portfolio structure and believe it will continue to appreciate in value as 2025 progresses.

The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio Dividend Section

Here's how much dividend income is generated per investment basket:

  • ETFs $577.09 (28.71%).
  • Equities $511.69 (25.46%).
  • REITs $355.63 (17.69%).
  • CEFs $335.21 (16.68%).
  • BDCs $230.48 (11.47%).

Collecting dividends can serve many functions in a portfolio. Some investors utilize dividends to supplement their income and live off of them. I'm building a dividend portfolio for myself 30 years into the future. In 2022, I collected $507.80 in dividend income from 533 dividends. In 2023, I collected $978.11 in dividend income from 660 dividends. 2024 was a great year as the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio generated $1,623.14 in dividend income from 709 dividends. After 7 weeks into 2025, $309.11 of dividend income was produced, which is 19.04% of the total income from 2024. There were 88 positions that paid a dividend, which is 12.41% of the dividends generated in 2024.

When I look back at the earlier years, I find it amazing how much progress has been made. In the first 7 weeks of 2025, I averaged $44.16 in dividend income. This is an increase of 41.47% or $12.94 compared to my weekly average of $31.21 in 2024. At this point last year, I had generated $163.59 compared to $309.11. I am very excited to get through Q1 so I can do a YoY comparison, as it will help me model out what to expect throughout the remainder of 2025. I do think my original goals for 2025 may have been a bit conservative, and after Q1, I may be updating them depending on what the data indicates.

We're halfway through February, and I have already produced $71.53 in dividend income, which is 73.77% of the total dividend income generated last February. A lot of dividend income is generated at the end of the month due to a lot of monthly CEFs and ETFs being paid. I have no idea where the final number will land, but I expect it to be north of $125. This will be a good indication of what will occur next month, as February and March were the only 2 months in 2024 where I didn't exceed $100 of dividend income. I am still a bit shocked that I already exceeded $200 in January, and there is certainly a chance that the largest month for 2025 could exceed $225 or $250 of dividend income.

This week, the NEOS NASDAQ-100(R) High Income ETF (QQQI) increased by 1.59%, which caused it to no longer generate at least 1 share of its dividend income. There are now 39 positions generating at least 1 share per year from their dividends, and the additional shares are expected to produce $175.52 in additional dividend income. I have started working on some positions that are not in the green section of the table below, and I need to try to allocate at least 50% of the capital I am deploying each week to these positions so I can round out the portfolio further. I knew that I would be a bit top-heavy, and while I am not exceeding a 5% allocation toward a single position, I want to diversify further into my smaller positions so they generate at least 1 share per year from their dividends.

The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio Composition

ETFs and REITs are still under 20% of the portfolio, but I can see either sector exceeding 20% for a minimal amount of time. After doing some research, I found that there are several REITs I want to increase my exposure to because I feel they are very undervalued. In the next several weeks, I will probably increase my exposure to the oil & gas and technology sectors as there are some interesting opportunities in those spaces. Individual equities now represent 37.73% of the Dividend Harvesting portfolio while generating 25.46% of the dividend income. REITs, ETFs, CEFs, and BDCs make up 62.27% of the portfolio and generate 74.54% of the forward income. I am working on getting individual equities to represent more of the portfolio. The long-term goal is to have the sectors be close to equal weight, but that may not happen for 5–10 years at this rate, if ever.

The top-10 positions continue to decline a bit, but I still want Altria Group, Inc. (MO) to get under 4% of the portfolio. I think that it's a big accomplishment that none of the positions are close to 5%, and only 4 positions exceed 3% of the portfolio. I am happy that the allocations are becoming more even when I look at the Top 20 positions. One of my long-term goals is to get this chart to flatten out further. That way, it will be easier for me to make investments in larger positions in the future. I think there are a lot of opportunities throughout the portfolio and in the top 20 positions. I will likely be adding capital toward some of these over the next several months.

The PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund (PDI) was replaced by the BlackRock Science and Technology Trust (BST) in the top-10 holdings and Realty Income Corporation (O) paid a dividend this week. The data for the top-10 holdings changed a bit because of this. My allocation to the top 10 positions is $6,037.28, and they finished the week with a value of $7,921.79. This is an ROI of $1,884.51 (31.21%). There has been $943.33 of dividends generated and reinvested, which is 15.63% of the initial investment into these positions. I'm now projecting that $545.48 of dividend income will be generated from the top 10 holdings, which have a forward yield of 9.04%. These positions represent 29.75% of the portfolio, while their forward dividend income is projected to generate 27.14% of the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio's annualized income.

Week 207 Additions

In week 207 I added to my positions in Pfizer Inc. (PFE), Starwood Property Trust, Inc. (STWD), and the Reaves Utility Income Trust (UTG).

Pfizer

  • I think that there is a big opportunity in PFE, considering it is trading at 8.64 times 2025 earnings and generating a 6.74% yield.
  • PFE has increased the dividend for 14 years and has a low dividend payout ratio of 54.02%.
  • When I look at the cash flow statement, PFE is generating $11.26 billion in cash from operations and $8.23 billion in free cash flow (FCF).
  • I think that PFE is generating too much FCF and EPS to be stuck at a single-digit P/E, and I think that it's undervalued for the amount of profitability it produces.

Starwood Property Trust

  • STWD is trading close to its book value of $19.39 and has a dividend yield of 9.7%.
  • I think STWD is a buy under $20, and I continue to add to my position when shares are yielding over 9%.
  • I think that STWD will be rewarded in 2025 for all the investments they made when the real estate sector fell out of favor.
  • STWD has maintained its dividend throughout difficult economic environments, and I am expecting the dividend to remain intact going forward.

Reaves Utility Income Trust

  • I am continuing to purchase UTG because of the amount of CapEx being spent to build new data centers.
  • UTG is yielding over 6.5%, and it's a diversified way for me to gain exposure to the utility sector.
  • I think that the demand for energy will continue to increase over the next several years as we are in a cloud computing and artificial intelligence arms race, and UTG will appreciate while generating a large amount of income.

Week 208 Game Plan

I have been trying to diversify away from REITs, but next week I am likely to add to my positions in NNN REIT, Inc. (NNN), VICI Properties Inc. (VICI), and Rithm Capital Corp. (RITM).

Conclusion

The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio is firing on all cylinders despite having to exit 3 positions in 2025. New milestones continue to be achieved, and the portfolio is now producing over $2,010 of forward dividend income, which allows me to make additional investments each week by reinvesting the dividends. In the beginning, when I started this portfolio with $100, it seemed like achieving results like this would take forever, but they didn't. Anyone can build an income-producing portfolio from the ground up without needing a lot of seed capital. Over the past 206 weeks, the dividend income being produced has increased by $2,002.65, going from $7.44 in week 1 to $2,010.59 in week 207. I am excited about the future as there are still many opportunities on the horizon, and I think everyone will be surprised when the portfolio finishes at the end of this year. Please leave all of your comments and suggestions below. I look forward to interacting with everyone in the comment section.

data.title: Experian: In Place Of Over-Exuberance, Cautious Optimism
data.description:
Not just a credit bureau or a data provider, Experian (LSE:EXPN) (OTCQX:EXPGF)(OTCQX:EXPGY) is both, and more. This value-add — alongside international diversification — is the reason why the company has thrived so far, despite macroeconomic headwinds, and is widely expected to continue doing well. The financials have been steady, but the price run-up looks unsustainable.

Trading update

Quarter 3 of FY2025 (ended 31 December 2024) delivered positive results, predictably so. Revenue grew by 6% at constant currency (‘organic’ revenue by 8% excluding variable data breach services) — almost as much as in H1 FY25, when headline growth was 7%.

Stable growth is exactly what investors appreciate about Experian. (Foreign exchange, naturally, is always a factor in a multinational; for Q3, the FX impact came to minus 2%.) The full-year forecast is a similar mid single-digit growth. The management also expects an improvement in operating margin of 30-50 basis points — close to an accretion of 60 bps reached in H1.

Growth by region, though, varied given vastly different economic situations. North America, the biggest market, is a solid performer (+6%); Latin America’s organic revenue added 8%; the UK and Ireland inched up by 1% only; the smallest contributor EMEA and Asia Pacific showed the best result with 9%.

Product performance too was affected by market location. B2B offerings, for example, led the growth in North America, while Latin America saw strength in Consumer Services. The Asia Pacific region benefited from the acquisition of illion, a data analytics firm.

Strategy

As an established player, Experian expends a lot of effort looking for new avenues of growth. And thanks to pervasive AI, it has had plenty to work with recently. Capitalizing on consumer data it holds, the company is focused on meeting the evolving needs of business clients in four “high-value” areas: credit & lending; identity verification & authentication; fraud risk management; and analytics software. Experian values the Total Addressable Market for B2B solutions at $90b.

Of course, to continue to be useful to business clients and maintain its competitive edge, Experian has to keep attracting consumer sign-ups. Free memberships — the base that fuels Experian’s product development and innovation initiatives — went up by 25% between FY19 and FY24, on the back of enhancements to services aimed at improving consumers’ financial lives.

Other than that, investments in artificial intelligence and data security are a big priority, in order to drive growth through technology but also to improve operational efficiencies. That is partly responsible for consistent margin improvements, which Experian expects to keep posting. At the same time, the goal is to achieve better capital efficiency, to keep a strong financial profile.

And that Experian already has. It maintains a reasonable amount of debt that is covered by operating cash flows (36%). The company flaunts a high cash conversion rate (H1 FY25: 97%) which implies operational efficiency and reliable income streams. Experian also boasts a return on capital employed of 17%; that augurs well for recent acquisitions, including that of Brazil’s ClearSale specializing in online fraud prevention.

Risks

As far as competition goes, Experian is the industry leader with the overall data moat. But the technological race is particularly intense. Although Experian does have an edge in key products with features like machine learning credit decisioning and device-based scoring, its R&D spend is a bit too conservative, for its scale. The annual budget (~$300m, or 4% of revenue, in 2024) is less than half of Equifax’s ($850m, 15% of revenue), the closest peer. Even though Experian is more diversified, in revenue sources and geographically, Equifax has already overtaken it in some aspects: in near complete migration to cloud — a move that comes with a number of advantages — and AI integration in most products.

As one would expect, macroeconomics is a chief worry in the credit reporting business. Regardless of economic gyrations, Experian has so far been able to handle cyclical rate pressures quite well. Not that it doesn’t take losses; in H1 FY25, underlying earnings (statutory profit before tax) decreased by 6% because of ‘adverse interest rate movements’.

Regulatory challenges are amounting, though. The ongoing lawsuit by the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau in the US could put the company back hundreds of millions of dollars. Additionally, major incidences of data breach, the latest one being in South Africa last year, expose the company to more regulatory scrutiny, on top of reputational risk.

Valuation

The stock has run up considerably, and quite a bit just in the last month since the publishing of the trading update on January 15. So it is no surprise that Experian is historically overvalued. At a P/E of 41x, LSE:EXPN is the highest it has been in four years. However, the other Big Three peers, Equifax (EFX) and TransUnion (TRU) are even more expensive, at 51x and 68x respectively — an obvious case of the American premium, in my eyes.

EXPGF: Momentum

Experian’s dividend, yielding 1% at a payout ratio of 46%, is too insignificant to pay attention to. It has been growing, so there is some hope.

Conclusion

Experian is enviably steady. From FY20 to FY24, revenue, operating earnings, operating cash flows and earnings per share all recorded a CAGR growth of 8%. This speaks volumes about execution, but the management is coming under increasing pressure to keep the performance up. In the near term, it should not be that difficult. After all, the company is the de facto industry leader. But not a faraway leader. And this warrants caution, since the competitor right behind appears more driven to outdo itself and the front-runner, too.

That aside, Experian is a defensive growth holding, resilient through economic cycles. If the price pulls back a bit from recent highs, long-term investors may want to accumulate shares.

data.title: What To Do After Another Bumble Stumble?
data.description:
I started adding shares of Bumble Inc. (NASDAQ:BMBL) to my portfolio in the low-mid teens in late 2023. I've long been doubtful about the durability of online dating platforms (what used to work in the dating world often doesn't work anymore), and I was specifically trigger-shy coming out of the pandemic. Just as people plotted "revenge travel" after being stuck in their own cities for an extended period, I felt that a period of "revenge dating" (not to mean an attempt to get revenge on your ex) was also likely to occur. I reasoned that the last thing people would want to do is sit online and swipe profiles when they had spent nearly 100% of their time online for a couple of years.

Nonetheless, Bumble appealed to me as an investor for a couple of reasons. First, it remained differentiated due to the platform's female-first contact structure. Second, I was weak at the knees for the company's $150m+ of annual free cash flow.

I remained a shareholder as the bottom dropped out from the stock in early-August 2024. Shares fell by as much as 40% on August 8, and I was likely one of the few investors who picked up more shares of BMBL below $5 that day (the stock closed at $5.71). I didn't see enough weakness in those Q2 results to invalidate the free cash flow thesis I had been clinging to. Ultimately, it paid off to average down on the panic.

I layered out of my trade in several chunks, the last of which was in the $9 range. I counted my lucky stars to have not realized a big net loss. Giving up on the cash flow story wasn't easy, especially as other analysts relished it, but I simply didn't have enough confidence in a business rebound. Ultimately, I reallocated my capital to better opportunities.

I was certainly tempted to layer back into a BMBL position as the stock dipped into the mid-$7 range in January, but never ended up pulling the trigger. Given the company's Q4 results and 2025 guidance released on February 18th, 2025, I'm pretty happy that I didn't.

Bumble's Q4 Results and Guidance Deliver Another Disappointment

On Tuesday, Bumble reported their Q4 results (or most of them). Revenue fell more than -4% for the quarter as compared to Q4 2023, and that's the worst comparable top-line result the company has seen. Expectations were pretty low, though, and Seeking Alpha reports that revenue slightly beat consensus.

Interestingly, Bumble was not able to provide a net earnings result for Q4 and FY 2024 due to what it described as accounting for foreign currency impacts had not yet been finalized. Whatever the ultimate result, it's an incremental concern that the company could not file a complete set of numbers.

What we do know is that profitability weakened, as average users increased about +6% against a ~9% decrease in average revenue per user.

As Bumble likes to present Adjusted EBITDA numbers, I'm always keen to have a look at the reconciliation tape, but the details aren't fully available for Q4 2024. Thankfully, Bumble did provide an operating income number of $36.97 million and an adjusted EBITDA of $72.51 million for the quarter. However, I fully consider Stock-Based Compensation [SBC] a real cost, so I'm not going to buy this adjusted EBITDA number. Instead, I come to a result of $58.53 million for the quarter, and $277.86 million for the year. On that basis, the deterioration in Q4 becomes more clear.

The guidance provided by the company in their conference call is also very disappointing. For FY 2025, management expects a revenue decline of 7%-10%. Margins are also expected to suffer. On the basis of the company's adjusted EBITDA calculation (which I don't agree with), their EBITDA margin for Q4 was about 27.7%. Management is expecting the apples-to-apples EBITDA margin for Q1 2025 to come in around 25%, which would be a decline of some 270 basis points. CFO Anu Subramanian attributed the expect decline to both revenue headwinds as well as investment costs. Earlier in the call, outgoing CEO Lidiane Jones indicated the company had new plans to innovate, and obviously some increased costs will be attributed there.

Cash Flow Statement Guesswork

I'm disappointed (and surprised) that Bumble did not provide cash flow information for the recent quarter. In the SEC filing that was available, the company stated that the delayed finalization of foreign currency impacts would have no impact on cash flows, yet the company failed to provide the cash flow details for Q4.

The company did report that its cash balance as of December 31, 2024, was $204.3 million and that total debt was $617.1 million, for a net debt balance of $412.8 million. However, these numbers seem awfully surprising.

As of September 30, 2024, I see Bumble had net debt of around $365 million. This would suggest that net debt increased by $48 million over Q4! The company did spend $40.3 million on share repurchases over the quarter, but that still doesn't get us back to even.

Based on these totals, it looks like Bumble either significantly ramped up its capex in Q4 or may have experienced negative operating cash flow during Q4 2024 due potentially to working capital adjustments.

Whatever occurred at the bank in Q4 seems to raise significant doubt about the cash flow story that had driven my previous investment interest.

Valuation

You'll forgive me for using back-of-the-napkin estimates as I try to figure out BMBL's valuation on a forward basis.

Using my own adjusted EBITDA number for Q4 2024 (which removed SBC add back) and then factoring in a top-line decline of 8% and a margin decline of 270 bps (as guided for Q1) brings me to an approximate EV/EBITDA of about 5.8x based on BMBL's February 19 closing price of $5.70.

This multiple remains a substantial discount to where Match Group (MTCH) is trading. However, I'm working with a number of unknown variables.

Concluding Remarks and Recommendation

Bumble's Q4 2024 release has seemingly left investors with a lot of disappointment and open questions, including:

  • What are the "innovations" that Bumble plans to introduce in an attempt to revitalize its business?
  • Will Bumble's adjusted EBITDA bottom in Q1 2025 at 25%, or will it continue to run at that level or lower through 2025?
  • How much did Bumble spend on capex in Q4 2024, and does this explain the surprising increase in net debt?
  • How should investors react to the company's failure to deliver full audited financials for Q4 2024?
  • What will the return of founder Whitney Wolfe Herd mean for Bumble's future, and has the company "kitchen-sinked" Q4 and sandbagged guidance to offer her an easy baseline?

Shares of BMBL fell nearly -20% in after-hours trading on February 18th but lost an additional $1 during the Feb 19th trading session, following the (partial) results and forward guidance. I'm not at all surprised by the very negative session after investors had the chance to unpack the information (and missing information) released by Bumble.

I wouldn't be surprised if some Wall Street analysts simply suspend their price targets for BMBL amidst the growing list of unknowns.

Using my back-of-the-napkin (my adjusted) EV/EBITDA multiple of ~5.8x based on BMBL's after-hours trading price, at face value BMBL may look reasonably priced for investors with a value/contrarian mindset.

With that said, the whole story looks substantially worse than it did back in August 2024. That's when the stock briefly slipped below $5. Therefore, I, personally, can't imagine taking a flyer on BMBL unless shares trade meaningfully below that level. The best hope for the bulls is that Bumble has given Ms. Wolfe Herd a story she can't make worse. I could see myself perhaps making a gamble on Bumble if shares drop below $4.

To therefore answer the "what to do?" question in the title, I think the answer is Sell, or Pray.

Manager Commentary

“Looking ahead to 2025, we believe market dynamics appear poised for rotation. While elevated valuations and narrow leadership raise caution, we think opportunities may emerge in value, cyclicals, and small caps as economic momentum broadens.” - Westfield Capital Management Company, L.P.

Market In Review

The S&P 500 reached 57 record highs in 2024, marking one of its best years since 1928, with over 20% annual returns for the second consecutive year. This remarkable performance was fueled by resilient U.S. economic growth, easing inflation, and enthusiasm for artificial intelligence (“AI”)-driven innovation. Large-cap growth stocks, led by the "Magnificent 7," continued to dominate, while small caps posted their worst relative performance since 1998. The U.S. Federal Reserve’s (“Fed”) pivot to rate cuts, along with optimism surrounding deregulation and fiscal reforms following the election, added momentum to a strong year, despite persistent concerns about market breadth and valuation extremes.

Portfolio Performance

During the fourth quarter of 2024, the Harbor Small Cap Growth Fund (Institutional Class, “Fund”) returned –2.87%, underperforming the Russell 2000® Growth Index, which returned 1.70%.

From a sector perspective, Consumer Discretionary, Information Technology, and Industrials were the primary detractors from relative performance. The underperformance was due to a combination of stock-specific weakness and common factor headwinds. From a factor perspective, the Fund’s underweight exposure to volatility and overweight exposure to size was a relative headwind to performance, which offset a relative tailwind from being underweight leverage.

Contributors & Detractors

Celestica (CLS), an electronics manufacturing services company, was the top contributor during the quarter after reporting a beat and raise in its third-quarter earnings report, as the company has benefited from its exposure to AI hyperscale customers. We sold our position during the quarter after the stock hit our internal price target.

Procore Technologies (PCOR), a cloud-based construction management software company, also contributed positively to relative returns after the company reset 2025 financial expectations and subsequently introduced new, long-term margin targets above expectations. We continue to favor Procore based on the potential for growth reacceleration and significant margin expansion.

Option Care Health (OPCH), a home health care and infusion provider, was the top detractor from relative returns during the quarter. The stock sold off following the company’s third-quarter earnings report in which management indicated a faster-than-anticipated decline in profit margin when a large portfolio drug goes generic in 2025. Despite the recent headline controversy, we maintain conviction in the company’s advantaged position as the only national home infusion provider that is taking market share in a growing industry, with the potential for additional upside from Alzheimer's drug-at-home infusions.

During the quarter, Vaxcyte (PCVX), a novel vaccine developer, also detracted from relative performance after the stock sold off over concerns about a new political administration and the potential impact on vaccine companies. In our view, the new administration will not have any impact on Vaxcyte’s franchise, and we believe the company could have a direct line of sight to a long-duration, total addressable market, which might represent a strategic asset for Big Pharma vaccine players.

Buys & Sells

During the quarter, we purchased Genius Sports (GENI), a leader in sports data analytics for global sports leagues and betting operators. Genius operates in a duopoly industry with secular tailwinds from the sports betting industry's 10% compound annual growth rate. We believe the company should benefit from long-term, exclusive contracts (including a multi-year NFL extension) and is approaching profitability, supported by its high-margin BetVision platform and growing advertising segment.

During the quarter, we sold Ciena (CIEN), a network and communication infrastructure company, after the stock hit our internal price target. In addition, the communication equipment space had a big move, and we were worried about fiscal year guidance in the new year.

Sector Overweights & Underweights

Financials represented the Fund’s largest overweight relative to the index at the end of 2023 and continues to be the largest overweight as of year-end 2024. Our exposure is heavily weighted toward growth-at-a-reasonable-price investment opportunities with idiosyncratic growth stories and limited interest rate risk. Across the sector, we are overweight property and casualty insurance with exposure to a few niche stories, modestly overweight banks, and underweight asset managers.

Consumer Staples represented the Fund’s largest underweight at the end of 2023 and continues to be the largest underweight as of year-end 2024. We have historically struggled to identify exciting growth opportunities in this group, and the resultant underweight has been characteristic of Fund positioning over time.

Outlook

For 2025, we believe market dynamics appear poised for rotation. While elevated valuations and narrow leadership raise caution, we think opportunities may emerge in value, cyclicals, and small caps as economic momentum broadens. The Fed’s accommodative stance, coupled with fiscal stimulus, support a constructive outlook in our view, but risks surrounding inflation and policy missteps remain key concerns. For these reasons, we believe a disciplined approach emphasizing high-quality investments and valuation sensitivity will be essential to navigating this evolving landscape.

Retirement Class shares commenced operations on March 1, 2016. The performance attributed to the Retirement Class shares prior to that date is that of the Institutional Class shares. Performance prior to March 1, 2016 has not been adjusted to reflect the lower expenses of Retirement Class shares. During this period, Retirement Class shares would have had returns similar to, but somewhat higher than, Institutional Class shares due to the fact that Retirement Class shares represent interests in the same portfolio as Institutional Class shares but are subject to lower expenses.

Expense ratio information is as of the Fund's current prospectus, as supplemented. Gross expenses are the Fund's total annual operating expense.

Performance data shown represents past performance and is no guarantee of future results. Past performance is net of management fees and expenses and reflects reinvested dividends and distributions. Past performance reflects the beneficial effect of any expense waivers or reimbursements, without which returns would have been lower. Investment returns and principal value will fluctuate and when redeemed may be worth more or less than their original cost. Returns for periods less than one year are not annualized. Current performance may be higher or lower and is available through the most recent month end at Active ETFs or by calling 800-422-1050.

Disclosures

All holdings-related data is provided by FactSet. Because FactSet relies on external sources for its data, that data may differ slightly from actual values maintained by Harbor Funds.

Due to the security valuation procedures of the Fund and intra-day trading activity not included in the FactSet calculations, the actual returns may vary. From time to time the cash return in the portfolio may appear distorted based on the way FactSet’s attribution calculation methodology addresses delayed settlements.

Beta is a rolling three year, unless the Fund has a track record of less than three years, in which case it is a rolling one year. Best and Worst Performers sections reflect stocks in the portfolio for the quarter with an average weight of 0.25% or greater.

Views expressed herein are drawn from commentary provided to Harbor by the subadvisor and may not be reflective of their current opinions or future actions, are subject to change without prior notice, and should not be considered investment advice.

This information should not be considered as a recommendation to purchase or sell a particular security. The weightings, holdings, industries, sectors, countries, and returns mentioned may change at any time and may not represent current or future investments.

As a result of changing market conditions, total net asset levels, expenses and other statistics may change at any time and may differ from those shown.

The total amount shown for sector, industries, or country holdings may be greater than 100% because of the inclusion of derivatives and the collateral securities supporting those instruments. Sector allocations are determined using the Global Industry Classification Standard (GICS), which is a service of Morgan Stanley Capital International (MSCI) and Standard & Poor's (S&P).

Investors should carefully consider the investment objectives, risks, charges and expenses of a fund before investing. To obtain a summary prospectus or prospectus for this and other information, visit Active ETFs or call 800-422-1050. Read it carefully before investing.

Attribution Disclosures

Linked Performance by Sectors data is produced from FactSet using data supplied by State Street Bank and Trust Company. Active Currency Contribution is the Currency Contribution of the portfolio minus the Currency Contribution of the benchmark.

Allocation Effect is the portion of portfolio excess return that is attributable to taking different group bets from the benchmark. (If either the portfolio or the benchmark has no position in a given group, allocation effect is the lone effect.) A group's allocation effect equals the average percent capitalization of the portfolio's group minus the average percent cap of the benchmark's group times the total return of the benchmark group minus the total return of the benchmark.

Average Weight is the dollar value (price times the shares held) of the security or group, divided by the total dollar value of the entire portfolio displayed as a percentage. It is calculated as the simple arithmetic average of daily values.

Contribution to Return is the contribution of a security or group to the overall portfolio return. It is calculated as the security weight multiplied by the daily security return linked daily across the reporting period. Currency Contribution is Total Return in USD subtracting out the Local Returns.

Local Returns are the Total Return of the portfolio or benchmark using the local currency.

Selection Effect is the portion of portfolio excess return attributable to choosing different securities within groups from the benchmark. A group's security selection effect equals the average weight of the benchmark's group times the total return of the portfolio's group minus the total return of the benchmark's group.

Total Effect is the sum of Allocation Effect and Selection Effect. The total effect represents the opportunity cost of what was done in a group relative to the overall portfolio. It is not just the difference between percent contribution in the portfolio and benchmark. At the overall portfolio level, the two numbers are equal. At the group level, they can be different.

Total Return is the price change of a security or group including dividends accrued over the report period (or the in-portfolio return) which includes only the time period that each security was in the portfolio.

Definitions

Beta is a measure of systematic risk, or the sensitivity of a fund to movements in the benchmark. A beta of 1 implies that the expected movement of a fund's return would match that of the benchmark used to measure beta.

Median Market Cap: The median size of the companies in a portfolio or index as measured by the market value of outstanding shares.

Weighted Average Market Capitalization: The average size of the companies in a portfolio or index as measured by the market value of outstanding shares. Earnings per share is the portion of a company's profit allocated to each outstanding share.

The Est 3-5 Yr EPS Growth (%) is the estimated growth of earnings per share over the next 3-5 years, using pre-calculated mean long-term EPS growth rate estimates, which are calculated using each individual broker’s methodology, from FactSet, First Call, I/B/E/S Consensus, and Reuters. Forward-looking estimates may not come to pass.

% EPS Growth - Past 3 Year: Earnings per share refers to the bottom-line measure of a company’s profitability defined as net income divided by the number of outstanding shares. The Adjusted Trailing P/E (price-to-earnings) Ratio is the closing stock price divided by the sum of the last 12 months actual EPS.

Forecasted P/E Ratio: a measure of the P/E (price-to-earnings) ratio using forecasted earnings for the P/E calculation. Return on Equity is a measure of financial performance calculated by dividing net income by shareholders' equity. The Price/Book (price-to-book) Ratio evaluates a firm's market value relative to its book value.

All P/E, ROE and P/B statistics are calculated as weighted medians.
